Honourable mention to GOTG, but it's so silly, I would I appreciated its design and fun more than I actually liked it. As for that phase, it's not over probably. Right now the vibe I'm getting is Old Man Blade, amirite??! lol, but in all seriousness, Disney has shown they can execute with certain properties and certain production teams. X-Men '97 for example, was done so well, and even despite Demayo's exit, I think it could still do well if they just listen to the fans. I think Doomsday will be a mixed bag, and F4 looks like a bit of a miss tbh. Everything just feels off. The Thing looks good, but talks normal lol. Everybody know's he's gotta have a bit of a voice switch to reflect his mutated tone. Then there's this movie, which is just... I don't know why they're rolling out Dlist characters when they could've just done a Storm movie to satisfy their DEI hunger. Or Rogue. Or any female X-Men. They've had the rights for years, they're just too scared and stupid to make anything yet. It's a bad sign yeah, but Disney, barring Avengers' failure, has still one last chance. X-Men live action. People love the X-Men and mostly fondly remember the Singerverse. If they fumble that, then I can't seem them recovering Marvel-wise. Even still, there's still DC and independent comics. Hell, I've been waiting for a new Spawn movie since I was a kid in the 90s and DC is so intent on releasing movies, that they don't even care if they suck or fail, so we will get at least a good DC film once in a while, even if it's a vomited diamond. I still haven't seen The Batman lol, but I've heard it's good. I just hate Zoe Kravitz? Is that her name?

Now if you're referring to the one-of-kind cinematic phase that includes horrible CGI oversaturation and really cheap, dated humour punctuated by quips that somehow almost never land, and if they do, never twice... then I am all for that as that was mostly trash anyways and without Whedon or Russos, Disney would've mangled all it touched. I'm all for the best elements of Nolan, Singer and Raimi to return to the genre, and none of them had quips. Russo brothers seem to really do well in this field when they're not forced to include Captain Marvel randomly lmao. I think they could stick a landing with Doomsday, and considering how much I loved Singer's X-Men vision pre-Apocalypse, I'm really hoping they do. I had the original X-Men on VHS so there's nostalgia there, as well as just being a huge X-Men fan before and after. I was convinced Disney would butcher X-Men '97 but I was completely wrong at least for Season 1 and I was never more happy to be wrong. I just loved that experience and I am both pissed and worried S2 is taking forever. So I will give them a chance with Doomsday. As for Thunderbolts... i may even see it as I haven't watched a Marvel movie in theatres since Endgame lol.

replies.author: @marraduse2519
replies.text:
​@theredguy8746 seriously?  Marvel superhero films are *not* the correct place for movies about mental health!  As someone else said, if I want that, I'll go and watch One Flew Over The Cuckoo's Nest.  The clue is in the word *superheroes* - they are uber heroic, not mentally ill.  So this is false packaging, drawing in the audience with the Marvel logo and on the back of *genuine* superhero films like Iron Man, and Thor, and then switching to a totally different tone.  You may have liked the movie but are you *fairly* and *objectively* judging the film *overall* which includes how it was marketed and therefore to whom it is aimed?  I think you aren't.  

You're judging it according to *your own* taste without *any* thought for the context of the Marvel fanbase and the previous style of films.  It's supposed to be a *franchise* .  I assume you recognise this fact but it seems you don't understand (or care) about what that means i.e. that the product fits *one* particular brand.  You don't go to McDonald's expecting to buy a five course meal sitting down at a table served by a waiter.  If, when you got to see the menu, you found that that was what was on offer, you'd *have* to feel disappointed and turn around to go and look for unhealthy 'fast' food elsewhere - *unless you didn't really care for what you got so long as it filled up your stomach* .  

That is, I suspect, the type of customer you represented because you were obviously quite happy to be served something different, quite prepared to take a gamble.  In short, you *aren't* a serious Marvel franchise customer.  Nerdrotic's critique is aimed at *serious* Marvel customers - like himself.  He and his fans grew up on Marvel and DC etc. comics and knows what he wants from films based on these.  He has every right to criticise the woke garbage Disney is churning out according to its woke agenda.  He's warning people who *need* the warning.  You don't because you aren't bothered what you get as long as it entertains *you* .   

Imagine you are a Marvel fan who actually suffers from depression.  You don't need or want anyone explaining to you what it's like because you *know* what's it's like all too well.  What you go to Marvel films for is the joyful escapism *to ESCAPE from your normal depressing existence* - and so you go to see The Thunderbolts hoping for a great movie like so many in your beloved *franchise* , and instead of what you wanted - and had every right to expect - you got some DEPRESSING film about mental illness.  Now, tell me that you enjoyed the film!  You wouldn't.  You'd be pissed off.  Lots of people are pissed off.

NB. That the trailer even says, "The best since Endgame" which implies that it will be similarly good - *SIMILARLY* good.  There's also nothing in the trailers to suggest what the film is really about.  No, Florence Pugh stepping off the top of a tall building does *not* suggest a suicide attempt - not in the context of Superhero films in which characters do all manner of dangerous things *casually* e.g. Iron Man walking calmly towards the camera as his weapons explode behind him.  He *appears* to be unconcerned and even uninvolved but the opposite is true.  No, Pugh's step off that ledge could have been illustrating her totally fearless focus on the job whereby she will free fall to a set point and then fire a grappling hook and swing into a window et al.

Disney is out of order and deserves all the criticism it gets.  Duck Fisney!

replies.author: @tumbleweedjoe
replies.text:
​@TankHunter678 That's a fair assessment. I really liked the movie, nd think people would find it engaging. Black Widow and Sentry's actors were very, very good. I found Red Guardian's shtick over the top but he had a certain charm. Could care less for ghost. I at least liked walker better and his fights were good. The action in general was solid, and there was less obvious cgi (except in certain parts). They actually made Sentry Sentry, and the way his story arc matches the theme and also makes sense in its resolution (given that there's no way any superhero, including Henry Cavill's Superman could match him. He is truly godlike.) 

I hated a few scenes and did not like the Seinfeld chick. However, she was supposed to be obnoxious, so there's that. I am not looking forward to Fantastic Four and plan not to see it in theaters because they made Silver Surfer into a flat cheated claymation chick. However, that doesn't change the fact that this film was quite enjoyable. I saw it with my son in Imax. He's fourteen, I'm forty. We both liked it. Men were engaged with the action. Women were moved by the emotion. People were laughing at the jokes (some of which were stupid, some of which hit.) It was a pretty good movie and they seemed to be at least trying to not M-She-U it. It was extremely gratifying seeing fake Taskmaster get unceremoniously wasted. I call that fan service.

replies.author: @PeterDB90
replies.text:
I hard disagree with Gary on this one - except for the end being anti-climactic, the movie is great. Don't delude yourself into thinking that Phases 1-3 were immaculate, there were some pretty bad films back then (maybe not as garbage as some of the recent ones, but still bad). Look me in the eye and tell me that the following isn't true:
Captain America (Phase 1) was a cheerleader for most of the movie, it was boring
Iron Man 3 (Phase 2) had so many iron man suits that it completely diluted what Iron Man is supposed to be, and Pepper Potts was just annoying in it, the only good part of that movie was Tony Stark talking to that kid in whose garage he was trying to recoup. 
Thor: The Dark World (Phase 2) I don't even remember what happens in that movie because it was so forgettable.
Phase 3 was the only relatively flawless one, but even that one had Captain Marvel and Spider Man Far From Home, neither of which is "bad", just not great, they were severely mediocre. 

Thunderbolts is heads and shoulders above ANY of those movies.
Gary is saying this is the "best of the worst" except neglects to mention that the best of the worst is still MUCH BETTER than the worst of the best. It's a top 15 MCU movie for me, which sounds like nothing until you realize just how many MCU movies there are, it's definitely in the top good half, not the bottom bad half.

replies.author: @RageDaug
replies.text:
@PeterDB90 I'm not going to say all the P1-P3 movies were super awesome, though I enjoyed all of them other than Marvel. The fan base enjoyed them so much that every phase 3 movie except a couple were billion dollar products, with infinity war clocking in at over $2B and End game over $3B.  How does the last movie of the phases that supposedly fatigued us bring in $3B? 

Dr Strange and Ant Man & the Wasp both did less, "only" $600M, with the lemmings saying was due to "super hero fatigue", ignoring the fact that 8 movies after Dr. Strange and 3 movies after Ant Man brought in between $900M and $3B.

IMO, the fact that we can see the fluctuation in ticket sales between $600M and $3B in Phase 3, and in no particular order, tells me, people paid to see the good movies and were not as excited to see the not as good movies.

Regarding Thunderbolts, it can be a super awesome movie that still doesn't sell a lot of tickets due to bad movie fatigue. No one trusts Marvel to put out a good movie right now. So there's no hype. No excitement. There are tons of Super awesome movies that no one sees, because no one thought they would be good, and didn't bother to find out.

replies.author: @kicknowledgesmith8608
replies.text:
​@ The tier of the characters isn't the issue. Iron man was B tier at the start, so was Thor and Cap. Guardians were C tier. This movie was good, the issue here is past missteps hurt it. Many lost trust.  Also, there is no nostalgia or gimmicks here.

 If this was 7 or 8 years ago, it would be doing better, that was a time when audiences flocked to anything Marvel put out. Dead pool and NWH are popular characters that already proved themselves prior to Endgame.  plus dead pool had Wolverine, none of those movies made a billion before that one. Same with Guardians 3. That was well received in the earlier phases,.  Many went to Love n Thunder for Thor but it was a disappointment. Many went to MOM because many were still high from NWH. 

Doomsday will most likely get away with featuring lower tier characters because of the Avengers name and all the nostalgia.

replies.author: @my-merethoughts5742
replies.text:
@kicknowledgesmith8608 The true issue is Disney emasculated the male superheroes or turned them into jokes. Secondly, they changed the sex, race and persona of the characters most marvel fans have grown to love. 

Lastly, the writing has taken a major hit. We came to see a lot of action, less jokes and a world level threat. Nobody asked for a Marvel movie centered around depression and trauma for 2 hours. You have to satisfy the masses and stick with what has worked in the past. Go back to before End Game started and use that formula.

replies.author: @kicknowledgesmith8608
replies.text:
@my-merethoughts5742 I hear you and you make some valid points  but I didn't get that from this movie. 

Bucky was bad ass, he took out those tanks and took down the Thunderbolts all by himself. The people voted for him to be congressman. He was respected by everyone, including Valentina assistant, and Valentina called him handsome. I didn't see him as being emasculated. 

Red Guardian is the typical Marvel humor but he delivered some heart as well at times. I actually liked him better in this movie than Black Widow. 

John Walker is one of  my favorite characters and I liked him in this movie. He was struggling with not  being Captain America. He's the complete opposite of Steve. Steve is perfect in the eyes of America. He can lift Thor's hammer. I think John is more relatable. I didn't see him as emasculated. He killed someone and it went viral, most Americans wouldn't be in favor of him being Captain America. As far as them calling him a jerk in the movie, he was, but mainly because of what he's dealing with in his personal life on top of losing the shield. Yelena even complimented him and said he knows what he's doing. I also think Ghost likes him. women tend to like that and she's not exactly good. They respect Walker at the end of the day. 

As far as Ghost gender change, I don't care because I didn't mind the Ancient One in Doctor Strange.  Also Ghost isn't super popular. If they gender swapped Steve Rogers that be different. They got rid of female Taskmaster,  that was good.

replies.author: @my-merethoughts5742
replies.text: @ You see the glass as half full, I see it differently. The only highlights in this movie belongs to Sentry. Disney nearly damaged that with OVER-COMMITTING to the mental health aspect of the movie. Allow that to be Sentry's storyline...it doesn't have to be all inclusive. A superhero movie CENTERED around crying, hugging and interventions is pure emasculation. There's so many different genres of movies to watch & embrace those "feelings." Marvel isn't the home for that and the box office failure will support that.

replies.author: @kicknowledgesmith8608
replies.text:
@ I'm certainly not defending Disney, I have my issues with them. but I liked this movie. I agree Sentry was awesome. I liked Bucky scene with the tanks and I liked when they all fought each other. 

The box office is more so about past missteps and other factors. This happened with Born Again also. That series also felt like 2 different shows stitched together. Many lost trust, I get it. 

The office doesn't determine quality. MI7 flopped, so did Furiosa, Fall Guy and Ministry of ungentlemanly Warfare and those were all well received.  I liked Thunderbolts better than Dead pool,. This movie connected and advanced the story and the post credits leads right into the next movie. That worked for me. I'm sure Doomsday will do much better money wise, because of the Avengers name in the title, the nostalgia and gimmicks. Everyone has different opinions. If you went in wanting to hate this, and view everything as political that will be the result.

replies.author: @nick1635
replies.text: It really isn't a movie about mental health, that's REALLY overselling it. Its your standard formulaic by-the-numbers Marvel plot with the antagonist having the most shallow backstory about depression, and some of the characters are superficially a bit sad for brief moments when the plot suits it. Pixar movies have dealt with the subject of feelings with way more depth and nuance. Its your standard Marvel movie really, nothing in any way edgy or deep. I'd rate it a 6 out of 10. Its well constructed but you've seen it a thousand times before. If you've seen the trailer, you've seen the movie.

Endgame. Winter soldier. Daredevil Netflix punisher Netflix. Wolverine iron man all dealt with trauma therapy and depression.

 Iron man was suffering from trauma  after the events of New York which caused him to have panic attacks.

Daredevil talked to a priest about his issues. Dex was dealing with trauma and talking to a therapist listening to recordings in season 3. He also worked at a crisis hotline that helps prevent. 
Curtis  led a support group for veterans in the Punisher series. Sam Wilson led a support group at the VA in Winter Soldier. Wolverine was drinking and dealing with depression in the Wolverine movie and the Deadpool movie.  Steve Rogers led a support group at the VA in Endgame. Andrew Spider-Man was dealing with depression and anger in NWH and talked about not pulling his punches while getting choked up.  A lot of this stuff is relatable. 

This movie is about misfits that have dark past it was well done in this movie.  Many are liking the film.
